title.en: Volume 20: Prominent people from the past until the present
title.raw: 卷二十 古今人表 Volume 20: Prominent people from the past until the present

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「但次古人而不表今人者，其書未畢故也。」〉

translations.literal: Shigu says: "The reason it only arranges ancient people and does not chart present people is that the book was not yet finished."
translations.idiomatic: As Yan Shigu notes: "It lists only figures of antiquity and not contemporaries because this work was left unfinished."

sentences.zh: 自書契之作，先民可得而聞者，經傳所稱，唐虞以上，帝王有號謚。

translations.literal: Since the creation of writing, what can be heard of the former people, as named in the classics and commentaries, from Tang and Yu upward, is that emperors and kings had titles and posthumous names.
translations.idiomatic: From the time writing first appeared, what the classics and commentaries preserve about the earliest ages is that rulers from Yao and Shun back were remembered with formal titles and posthumous names.

sentences.zh: 輔佐不可得而稱矣，[1]而諸子頗言之，雖不考虖孔氏，然猶著在篇籍，歸乎顯善昭惡，勸戒後人，故博采焉。

translations.literal: Their assistants and supporters cannot be fully named. Yet the various masters discuss them somewhat; although these accounts are not verified by the Confucian tradition, they are still recorded in writings, and their purpose returns to making good manifest and evil clear, exhorting and warning later people, therefore I have collected them broadly.
translations.idiomatic: The chief ministers who aided those rulers cannot all be securely identified. Still, the various philosophical writings speak of them. Even when such accounts are not confirmed by the Confucian canon, they remain in the textual record and serve to highlight virtue, expose wrongdoing, and admonish later generations, so I have gathered them widely.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「若聖與仁，則吾豈敢？」

translations.literal: Confucius said: "As for being sage and benevolent, how could I dare claim that?"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "If you speak of sageliness and perfect benevolence, I would not presume to claim them."

sentences.zh: [2]又曰：「何事於仁，必也聖乎！」

translations.literal: He also said: "What is the matter with merely benevolence? It would have to be sageliness!"
translations.idiomatic: He also said, "Why stop at benevolence? One would have to be a sage."

sentences.zh: [3]「未知，焉得仁？」

translations.literal: "If one does not yet know, how can one attain benevolence?"
translations.idiomatic: "Without understanding, how could one realize true benevolence?"

sentences.zh: [4]「生而知之者，上也；

translations.literal: "Those who know from birth are the highest;
translations.idiomatic: "Those born with knowledge are the highest type;

sentences.zh: 學而知之者，又其次也；

translations.literal: those who know through learning are next;
translations.idiomatic: those who gain knowledge through study come next;

sentences.zh: 困而不學，民斯為下矣。」

translations.literal: those who are troubled yet do not learn, among the people these are the lowest."
translations.idiomatic: and those who face difficulty yet refuse to learn are the lowest among the people."

sentences.zh: [5]又曰：「中人以上，可以語上也。」

translations.literal: He also said: "With those above the middle grade, one may speak of higher things."
translations.idiomatic: He also said, "With people above the middle level, you can discuss higher teachings."

sentences.zh: [6]「唯上智與下愚不移。」

translations.literal: "Only the highest wisdom and the lowest stupidity do not change."
translations.idiomatic: "Only those of the highest wisdom and the deepest folly are unchangeable."

sentences.zh: [7]傳曰：譬如堯舜，禹、稷、禼與之為善則行，[8]鮌、讙兜欲與為惡則誅。

translations.literal: The Tradition says: for example with Yao and Shun, when Yu, Ji, and Xie joined them in doing good, it was carried out; when Gun and Huandou wished to join them in doing evil, they were executed.
translations.idiomatic: As one tradition puts it: under Yao and Shun, when Yu, Ji, and Xie promoted what was good, their policies were enacted; but when Gun and Huandou sought to push evil, they were punished with death.

sentences.zh: [9]可與為善，不可與為惡，是謂上智。

translations.literal: They can be joined in doing good, but cannot be joined in doing evil; this is called highest wisdom.
translations.idiomatic: If one can be led into good but never into evil, that is what is called the highest wisdom.

sentences.zh: 桀紂，龍逢、比干欲與之為善則誅，[10]于莘、崇侯與之為惡則行。

translations.literal: With Jie and Zhou, when Long Feng and Bi Gan wished to join them in doing good, they were executed; when Yu Xin and the Marquis of Chong joined them in doing evil, it was carried out.
translations.idiomatic: By contrast, under Jie and Zhou, loyal men like Long Feng and Bi Gan were killed for trying to guide their rulers toward good, while Yu Xin and the Marquis of Chong prospered by abetting evil.

sentences.zh: [11]可與為惡，不可與為善，是謂下愚。

translations.literal: Can be joined in doing evil, cannot be joined in doing good; this is called lowest stupidity.
translations.idiomatic: Those who can be drawn into evil but not into good are called the lowest fools.

sentences.zh: 齊桓公，管仲相之則霸，竪貂輔之則亂。

translations.literal: As for Duke Huan of Qi, when Guan Zhong served as his minister, he became hegemon; when Shu Diao assisted him, there was disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi became a hegemon under Guan Zhong, but fell into disorder when advised by Shu Diao.

sentences.zh: [12]可與為善，可與為惡，是謂中人。

translations.literal: Can be joined in doing good, can be joined in doing evil; this is called the middle type of people.
translations.idiomatic: Those who can be led toward either good or evil are what we call average people.

sentences.zh: 因茲以列九等之序，究極經傳，繼世相次，緫備古今之略要云。

translations.literal: Accordingly I arranged the sequence of nine grades, exhausting the classics and commentaries, following generations in succession, and in sum providing the essentials of antiquity and the present.
translations.idiomatic: On this basis, I set out a nine-rank order, drawing fully from the classics and commentaries, tracing each age in sequence, and assembling a concise outline of key figures from antiquity to later times.

sentences.zh: 〈張晏曰：「老子玄默，仲尼所師，雖不在聖，要為大賢，文伯之母達於禮典，動為聖人所歎，言為後世所則，而在第四。

translations.literal: Zhang Yan says: "Laozi was profound and silent, one whom Zhongni took as teacher; though not among the sages, he was surely a great worthy. Wenbo's mother was versed in ritual canons; her conduct was praised by sages, and her words were taken as models by later ages, yet she is placed in the fourth rank.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Yan comments: "Laozi was deep and reserved, and even Confucius learned from him. Though not classed as a sage, he was certainly a great worthy. Wenbo's mother was thoroughly versed in ritual, praised by sages for her conduct, and imitated by later generations for her words, yet she is only placed in the fourth class.

sentences.zh: 田單以即墨孤城復強齊之大，魯連之博通，忽於榮利，藺子申威秦王，退讓廉頗，乃在第五。

translations.literal: Tian Dan, with the lone city of Jimo, restored mighty Qi; Lu Lian was broadly learned and indifferent to glory and profit; Master Lin demonstrated authority before the King of Qin and yielded to Lian Po, yet they are placed in the fifth rank.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Dan revived powerful Qi from the isolated city of Jimo; Lu Zhonglian was widely learned and dismissed fame and gain; Lin Xiangru overawed the king of Qin and later yielded place to Lian Po, yet all are ranked only in the fifth class.

sentences.zh: 大姬巫怪，好祭鬼神，陳人化之，國多淫祀，寺人孟子違於大雅，以保其身，旣被宮刑，怨刺而作，乃在第三。

translations.literal: Great Ji was shamanistic and strange, fond of sacrificing to spirits; the people of Chen were transformed by this and the state had many excessive cults. The eunuch Mengzi departed from the Great Elegant norm to preserve his own body; after suffering castration, he wrote resentful and satirical works, yet is placed in the third rank.
translations.idiomatic: Tai Ji indulged in shamanic superstition and spirit sacrifices, and the people of Chen followed her into proliferating illicit cults. The eunuch Mengzi, straying from orthodox standards to save himself, later wrote resentful satires after his castration, yet is ranked in the third class.

sentences.zh: 嫪毐上烝，昬亂禮度，惡不忍聞，乃在第七。

translations.literal: Lao Ai committed incest with one above, confounding and violating ritual norms; the evil is unbearable to hear, yet he is in the seventh rank.
translations.idiomatic: Lao Ai committed unspeakable incest and threw ritual order into chaos, crimes too vile to recount, yet he is placed in the seventh class.

sentences.zh: 其餘差違紛錯不少，略舉揚較，以起失謬。

translations.literal: As for the rest, discrepancies and confusions are not few; I briefly raise and compare them to expose errors.
translations.idiomatic: There are many other inconsistencies and confusions; I mention and compare a few to highlight these mistakes.

sentences.zh: 獨馳騖於數千歲之中，旁觀諸子，事業未究，而尋遇竇氏之難，使之然乎？」

translations.literal: Alone he galloped through several thousand years, surveying the various masters from the side; his undertaking was not finished, and then he encountered the Dou-clan disaster. Did this cause it to be so?"
translations.idiomatic: He ranged alone across several millennia, examining the various schools, but before he could complete the project he was caught up in the Dou clan calamity. Is that why it ended this way?"

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「六家之論，輕重不同；

translations.literal: Shigu says: "The judgments of the six schools differ in what they treat as weighty or light;
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu says: "The six traditions evaluate things differently, assigning different weights and priorities;

sentences.zh: 百行所存，趣拾難壹。

translations.literal: Among the hundred kinds of conduct that remain, the aims and selections are hard to unify.
translations.idiomatic: With so many forms of conduct preserved in the record, selecting and ranking them by one standard is difficult.

sentences.zh: 張氏輒申所見，掎摭班史，然其所論，又自差錯。

translations.literal: Master Zhang then set forth his own views and criticized Ban's history, yet what he argued also had errors of its own.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang simply advanced his own opinions and faulted Ban Gu's history, but his own arguments were themselves inconsistent.

sentences.zh: 且年代乆遠，墳典隟亡，學者舛駮，師論分異，是以表載古人名氏，或與諸書不同。

translations.literal: Moreover, the ages are long remote, classic texts were lost in the cracks, scholars differ and refute, and masters' opinions are divided; therefore the names recorded in this table may in some cases differ from other books.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, those eras are very distant, many classical records were lost, scholars disagree and dispute, and learned traditions diverge. For that reason, some names in this table differ from what appears in other texts.

sentences.zh: 今則特有發明，用暢厥旨。

translations.literal: Now there is particularly this clarification, used to make its intent clear and expansive.
translations.idiomatic: This note is offered specifically to clarify and bring out the underlying intent.

sentences.zh: 自女媧以下，帝鴻以前，諸子傳記，互有舛駮，敘說不同，無所取正，大要知其古帝之號而已。

translations.literal: From Nüwa downward and before Dihong, the various masters' records and biographies mutually contain errors and contradictions, with differing narratives and no source to take as correct; the main point is only to know the names of those ancient emperors.
translations.idiomatic: For the era between Nüwa and before Dihong, the various textual traditions and biographies conflict with one another and offer no single authoritative account; at most, we can preserve the names of those ancient sovereigns.

sentences.zh: 諸人士見於史傳，彰灼可知者，無待解釋，其間幽昧者，時復及焉。」〉

translations.literal: As for figures whose deeds are seen in histories and biographies and are manifestly knowable, they need no explanation; among them, where there is obscurity, I at times further address it."
translations.idiomatic: For people whose deeds are clearly recorded in the historical sources, no explanation is needed; where points remain obscure, I occasionally add clarification."
